title: Eros
abstract: This course examines eros — the love of becoming one with something — as the first in Vervaeke's trilogy of loves (eros, philia, agape), each with a distinct logic. After completing this course, you will be able to articulate why eros is not reducible to sexual desire but encompasses any orientation toward union or consummation; why eros is satisfied by becoming one with its object rather than by cooperation or creativity; and how Socrates transformed eros into 'ta erotika' — an art of knowing what to care about and how to care about it rightly. Eros is essential to the series arc because it introduces the comparative analysis of love that makes the distinctiveness of agape — and the meaning-making power of different ways of loving — intelligible. Understanding eros also grounds the later discussion of how the erotic drive toward union can be sublimated or misdirected, driving both wisdom and foolishness.

modules.title: Eros as Consumptive Unification
modules.learningObjective: Articulate the logic of eros as the love that seeks to become one with its object, satisfied through consummation and union.

expositionPassages.phrase: Eros is the love that seeks to be one with something.
So this is the love that is satisfied through consummation. Phylia. This is the love that seeks cooperation. This is the love in which we experienced reciprocity.
expositionPassages.note: Eros contrasted with philia in terms of their distinct logics

expositionPassages.phrase: Eros is consumptive - making one with -, Phylia is cooperation - we work together.

socraticSeeds.prompt: Think about the difference between eating something delicious and working alongside a collaborator on a project. Both could involve care and positive feeling — but what is the fundamental difference in what each seeks?
expectedThemes: eating seeks to become one with
collaboration seeks ongoing cooperation
eros seeks consummation philia seeks reciprocity

socraticSeeds.prompt: Vervaeke illustrates eros with eating a cookie, sexual desire, and the desire to become one with nature. What do all three have in common that makes them structurally the same kind of love?
expectedThemes: all seek to dissolve the boundary between self and other
all are satisfied by union or consummation
all cease once union is achieved

socraticSeeds.prompt: Is eros always satisfied when it achieves union — or can you think of cases where the union creates new longing rather than fulfilling it? What does that suggest about the limits of erotic logic?
expectedThemes: some erotic fulfillment triggers new desire
eros may be intrinsically restless
points toward eros needing supplementation by philia or agape

socraticSeeds.prompt: If eros is satisfied by union and consummation, what happens to it once the beloved is fully 'had'? Does eros sustain a relationship, or does it require something else to keep a relationship alive?
expectedThemes: eros is fulfilled and thus concluded by consummation
relationships require philia and agape beyond eros
not self-sustaining over time

misconceptionBranches.misconception: Eros just means sexual love or physical desire.
misconceptionBranches.correction: In Vervaeke's usage, eros is any orientation whose logic is union or consummation — it includes sexual desire but equally applies to the desire to become one with nature, music, or even a delicious meal.

misconceptionBranches.misconception: Eros is the highest or most complete form of love.
misconceptionBranches.correction: Vervaeke presents eros, philia, and agape as three loves with distinct logics, none simply reducible to or superior to the others — he later argues that agape has a creative and sacrificial dimension eros lacks.

checkForUnderstanding.prompt: What is the distinctive logic of eros — what does it seek, and what satisfies it — and how does that contrast with philia's logic of cooperation?
expectedThemes: eros seeks union and is satisfied by consummation
philia seeks cooperation and reciprocity
different structures not just different intensities

modules.title: Ta Erotika: Socrates's Craft of Loving Rightly
modules.learningObjective: Explain what Socrates meant by mastery of 'ta erotika' — not just sexual knowledge but an art of knowing what to care about and how to direct erotic orientation toward wisdom.

expositionPassages.phrase: Socrates new/taught erotica, which wasn't just sex. Socrates knew what to care about.
expositionPassages.note: Vervaeke's identification of Socratic ta erotika with wisdom about care and orientation

socraticSeeds.prompt: Socrates claimed to know only one thing: ta erotika — the erotic things, or the art of love. What might it mean to be a master of 'what to care about' rather than a master of some body of knowledge?
expectedThemes: wisdom as orientation not information
knowing what matters vs. knowing facts
eros as an orienting force that needs calibration

socraticSeeds.prompt: If eros is the energy of longing and union, what would it mean to direct it poorly — toward things unworthy of it — versus to direct it rightly? Can you think of examples of misdirected eros?
expectedThemes: eros toward trivial or harmful objects
addictive patterns as misdirected eros
wisdom as proper calibration of erotic orientation

socraticSeeds.prompt: Plato's Symposium has Socrates reporting Diotima's ladder of beauty — eros can start with a beautiful body and be sublimated upward to beauty itself. What does this suggest about eros's potential as a vehicle for transformation?
expectedThemes: eros as anagogic energy
erotic drive can be redirected to larger objects
connection to philosophical ascent

socraticSeeds.prompt: Is 'knowing what to care about' a form of knowledge you could teach in a classroom? If not, what kind of knowing is it — and what practices might cultivate it?
expectedThemes: perspectival and participatory knowing
requires character formation not just information
Socratic dialogue as practice context

misconceptionBranches.misconception: Mastery of ta erotika is mastery of seduction — knowing how to attract others.
misconceptionBranches.correction: Vervaeke reads Socratic ta erotika as an art of knowing what is genuinely worth caring about and how to orient one's erotic energy toward it — it is fundamentally about wisdom and proper orientation, not seduction.

misconceptionBranches.misconception: Eros as a love philosophy is separate from cognitive function — it is purely about feeling and desire.
misconceptionBranches.correction: For Vervaeke, eros is inseparable from relevance realization — what one cares about structures what is salient and real; mastery of eros (ta erotika) is therefore mastery of a fundamental cognitive orientation.

checkForUnderstanding.prompt: What did Socrates mean by claiming mastery of ta erotika — and why does Vervaeke read this as a claim about wisdom rather than simply about erotic skill?
expectedThemes: ta erotika as knowing what to care about
orientation of erotic energy is a form of wisdom
eros is not only feeling but a structuring of what matters
